assets.name: lecture_02_figure_01.png
assets.rationale: None of the candidate frames are useful for lecture-note reconstruction. The window contains only Stanford campus exterior shots and a Stanford University title card, with no blackboard mathematics, diagram, lecturer-board interaction, or board layout evidence. Per the selection rules, these should be rejected.

assets.name: lecture_02_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the best balance of completeness and legibility. The transverse-energy expression on the right is already written clearly, and the new line beginning with "H =" is visible enough to capture the key interpretive move of the moment: re-reading the energy expression as a Hamiltonian. Earlier frames do not yet show that step, while the later frame adds only a partially written symbol with more ambiguity and no substantial gain in usable board content.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: Energy is Hamiltonian and Hamiltonian is i d by dt.
assets.caption_hint: Hamiltonian form of the boosted energy

assets.name: lecture_02_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: This is the clearest and most usable frame. It preserves the full discrete energy expression together with the sketch of the string as a chain of mass points, and unlike the earlier candidates it is essentially unobscured by Susskind's hand or arm. That makes it the best evidence frame for both the mechanical model and the equation on the board.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: Lagrangian is kinetic energy minus potential energy.

assets.name: lecture_02_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This frame best captures the actual transition to the Lagrangian form. It shows the continuum integral with the kinetic term already visible and the potential term being written with the crucial minus sign, while still preserving more of the surrounding board than the tighter final close-up. Earlier frames are less finished, and the latest frame crops too much of the left-hand structure.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: The Lagrangian is kinetic energy minus potential energy,

assets.name: lecture_02_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This is the most useful frame because the derivative \(\partial X/\partial \sigma\) is fully visible and the right-hand discrete increment is already being written, matching the spoken point that the difference becomes the derivative times \(\Delta\sigma\). Earlier frames stop before that replacement is explicit, while this one captures the key continuum-limit step despite the lecturer's hand still being present.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: becomes exactly, the derivative of x with respect to sigma times delta sigma.

assets.name: lecture_02_figure_06.png
assets.rationale: This is the clearest frame: Susskind has moved out of the way, the derivative expression across the top line is fully visible, and the lower quadratic mode expression is less occluded than in the earlier candidates. The later frame preserves the finished board state without adding blur or cropping.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: So, that's the derivative of x with respect to sigma.
assets.caption_hint: Mode expansion of the sigma derivative
